fields: Which part of the pituitary contains Herring bodies and pituicytes?

Posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis).
↳ Herring bodies store hormones, pituicytes provide structural support
What are the components of the posterior pituitary?
Pars nervosa, infundibular stalk, median eminence
What are the three parts of the anterior pituitary?
Pars distalis, pars intermedia, pars tuberalis
What is the embryological origin of the anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis)?
Rathke’s pouch (oral ectoderm)
Which cells in the anterior pituitary are acidophils, and what do they secrete?

Acidophils secrete simple proteins
↳ Cells include Somatotrophs (GH), Mammotrophs (Prolactin).

 

Mnemonic: GPA = Growth hormone, Prolactin, Acidophils.
What is the embryological origin of the posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis)?
Neuroectoderm of the diencephalon (hypothalamus)
Which hormones are secreted by basophils of the anterior pituitary?

FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH.



Mnemonic: B-FLAT = Basophils, FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH
What are chromophobes in the anterior pituitary?
Degranulated acidophils/basophils, stem cells, or follicular cells (non-secreting)
Which anterior pituitary part secretes melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH)?
Pars intermedia
What are the hormones of the posterior pituitary?

Oxytocin and ADH (vasopressin)
↳ synthesized in hypothalamus, stored in posterior pituitary
Which hypothalamic nuclei synthesize ADH and oxytocin?

ADH: Supraoptic nucleus
Oxytocin: Paraventricular nucleus
What are Herring bodies in the Pituitary?
Dilated axon terminals in the posterior pituitary that store ADH and oxytocin
What are pituicytes and where are they found?

Glial-like support cells in the posterior pituitary (pars nervosa)
↳ resemble astrocytes (BBB)
What are signs of hyperprolactinemia in females?

Amenorrhea, galactorrhea, decreased bone density (from low estrogen)



associated with dopamine loss
What condition results from GH excess in adults?
Acromegaly (gigantism)
What causes secondary hyperthyroidism in pituitary disease?
TSH-secreting adenoma from the pituitary (hyperpituitarism)
What is the most common cause of hypopituitarism?

Macroadenoma (tumor) compressing the pituitary gland, prevents secretion



i.e. tumor compression (Hypo-)
What are signs of FSH/LH deficiency in men?
Loss of libido, infertility, gynecomastia, loss of body hair
What happens in TSH deficiency (hypopituitarism)?
Weight gain, cold intolerance (lower basal metabolism), constipation, dry skin (hypothyroidism)
How is prolactin regulated?
Tonically inhibited by dopamine from the hypothalamus
What are examples of endocrine and exocrine glands?

Endocrine: pituitary, thyroid, adrenal
Exocrine: sweat, salivary glands, pancreas (exocrine portion)
What are peptide hormones synthesized as, and where in the cell?

Synthesized as preprohormones
↳ in the rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER)



i.e. protein synthesis
What is the precursor of insulin, and what is co-secreted with insulin?

Proinsulin
↳  C-peptide is co-secreted



C-peptide is useful as a biological marker (1 insulin = 1 c-peptide)
↳ packaged with insulin in secretory granules and released into the bloodstream during exocytosis
What are steroid hormones derived from?
Cholesterol
Where are steroid hormones synthesized and stored?

Synthesized on demand in adrenal cortex, gonads, and placenta
↳ not stored
Which hormones are derived from tyrosine?
Catecholamines (Epi, NE) and thyroid hormones (T3, T4)
How do thyroid hormones circulate in blood?

Mostly bound to thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG)
↳ only free T3/T4 are active
What is the half-life of free vs. bound hormones?

Free (peptide) hormones: short
Bound (steroid/thyroid) hormones: long



Remember free T3/T4 is easily degradeable. Binding (e.g., to TBG) extends hormone half-life .
What are the major second messenger systems used by peptide hormones?
cAMP, IP₃/DAG, Ca²⁺–Calmodulin, and cGMP
Which hormones act via the cAMP pathway?

FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH, PTH, glucagon, β1 agonists
↳ Anterior Pituitary

 

Remember, B-FLAT + PTH 
All act via Gs → ↑adenylyl cyclase → ↑cAMP → ↑PKA
Which hormones use the IP3/DAG (phospholipase C) pathway?

GnRH, TRH, GHRH, oxytocin, ADH (V1 receptor)
↳ Posterior pituitary
↳ Gq Proetin 

 

Remember all the releasing hormones "-RH" + post pit (oxytocin + ADH (V1))


remember, ADH V2 is cAMP !!
What is the major control mechanism for hormone secretion?
Negative feedback
What is the difference between short-loop and long-loop feedback?

Short-loop: tropic hormone inhibits hypothalamus

Long-loop: peripheral hormone inhibits both pituitary and hypothalamus
How do water-soluble hormones reach their target cells? How do lipid-soluble reach?

Water-soluble: dissolve in plasma, bind to membrane receptors, and use second messengers
↳e.g. Epinephrine, insulin


Lipid-solube: bind to transport proteins, enter cells, and alter gene transcription.
↳ e.g. T3/T4
What are two methods of measuring low hormone levels in the lab?
Radioimmunoassay (RIA) and ELISA.
What is the principle of radioimmunoassay?

Radiolabeled hormone competes with patient sample hormone for limited antibodies
↳ inverse relationship between radioactivity and hormone level
What is the carrier protein for ADH and oxytocin?

Neurophysin
↳ Neurophysin II for ADH
↳ Neurophysin I for oxytocin

 

carries ADH / Oxytocin from supraoptic / paracentricular nuclei to the Herring bodies for bloodstream secretion
Which hypothalamic hormone inhibits GH and TSH release?
Somatostatin (Growth Hormone Inhibiting Hormone, GHIH)
What is the main hormone produced by somatotrophs in the anterior pituitary?

Growth Hormone (GH) 
↳ acidophils (somatotrophs), adenohypophysis

 
 

50% of all produced
What is the signaling pathway used by the Growth Hormone receptor?

JAK2/STAT5 pathway

 

Remember, IGF-1 uses tyrosine kinase receptors (like insulin)!
What is the main mediator of GH’s effects on growth and metabolism?

IGF-1 (Insulin-like Growth Factor 1 / Somatomedin C)
↳ produced by the liver
What metabolic effects does GH have on glucose and fat metabolism?

increases the release of free fatty acids, and promoting gluconeogenesis in the liver.

↑ Lipolysis, ↓ glucose uptake (insulin resistance → diabetogenic effect)
What are the skeletal effects of GH in children vs adults?

Children: Linear growth (gigantism)

Adults: Bone thickening (acromegaly)
What factors increase GH secretion?
GHRH, exercise, sleep, stress, hypoglycemia, fasting, puberty (testosterone/estrogen)
What factors inhibit GH secretion?
Somatostatin, hyperglycemia, IGF-1 (negative feedback), obesity
What is the cause and presentation of Laron syndrome?

Dwarfism
GH receptor defect → low IGF-1, high body GH
What is the cause of Sheehan syndrome?
Postpartum ischemic necrosis of the pituitary (due to blood loss).
What are the major joints of the pelvis?
Pubic symphysis (anterior), sacroiliac joints (posterior), and lumbosacral joint (L5-S1).
Which ligaments form the greater and lesser sciatic foramina?

Sacrotuberous and sacrospinous ligaments



(VIVA)
What is the difference between the true and false pelvis?

False pelvis (greater pelvis) is above pelvic inlet
True pelvis (lesser pelvis) is below pelvic inlet and contains pelvic organs



False pelvis = from ASIS
True pelvis = from Sacrum
What defines the pelvic inlet?
A line from sacral promontory to pubic symphysis via the arcuate line (pelvic brim)
What defines the pelvic outlet?
Boundaries include pubic symphysis, ischiopubic rami, ischial tuberosities, sacrotuberous ligaments, and coccyx
What changes occur to the female pelvis during pregnancy?

Pelvic joints relax due to Relaxin (hormone)
↳ increasing the pelvic diameter (especially true conjugate)
What is the obstetric (true) conjugate and how is it measured?

Sacral promontory to posterior pubic symphysis
↳ cannot be directly measured in pelvic exam
What is the diagonal conjugate and why is it important?

Sacral promontory to inferior pubic symphysis
↳ measurable during pelvic exam to estimate obstetric conjugate
What are classic signs of acromegaly? How would you test acromegaly?

Symptoms: Frontal bossing, enlarged hands/feet, prognathism, organomegaly, kyphosis

 

Oral glucose suppression test (GH fails to suppress)
↳ also check IGF-1 levels
What is the treatment for acromegaly?

Transsphenoidal resection of pituitary tumor

↳ or Somatostatin analogs (e.g. octreotide)
What is the difference between the amino acid sequences of ADH and oxytocin (pituitary)?

Both are nonapeptides (9 amino acids) and share similar structures.
↳ They differ at positions 3 and 8
What structure passes through the obturator foramen?
Obturator nerve and vessels
What structures pass through the greater sciatic foramen?
Piriformis, superior/inferior gluteal nerves and vessels, sciatic nerve, pudendal nerve, internal pudendal vessels, posterior cutaneous nerve of thigh
What structures pass through the lesser sciatic foramen?
Tendon of obturator internus, pudendal nerve, internal pudendal vessels (re-entering pelvis)
What is spondylolysis?

Fracture of the pars interarticularis, commonly at L5

 

spondylo-lysis = Vertebra-break
What is spondylolisthesis and its clinical relevance to the pelvis?

Anterior displacement of vertebra due to bilateral pars defect
↳ can narrow pelvic outlet
What muscles form the pelvic diaphragm?

Levator ani (puborectalis, pubococcygeus, iliococcygeus) and coccygeus



Strengthened by kegel exercises
What are the functions of the pelvic diaphragm? What happens if the pelvic diaphragm weakens?

Supports pelvic viscera, maintains continence, and withstands intra-abdominal pressure.



Weakness causes pelvic organ prolapse (e.g., uterus, bladder, rectum), especially after childbirth
What are the four main arteries that enter the lesser pelvis?
Internal iliac arteries, gonadal arteries (testicular/ovarian artery), superior rectal artery, median sacral artery
What are the posterior division branches of the internal iliac artery?
Iliolumbar artery, lateral sacral arteries, superior gluteal artery
What are the anterior division branches of the internal iliac artery?
Umbilical, obturator, superior/inferior vesical (♂), vaginal (♀), uterine (♀), middle rectal, inferior gluteal, internal pudendal
What is the key difference between endocrine and exocrine glands in terms of secretion?
Endocrine glands secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream, while exocrine glands release their contents into ducts
What cancers arises from remnants of Rathke’s pouch?

Craniopharyngioma 
↳ may compress the optic chiasm and cause bitemporal hemianopsia
What are the embryologic origins of the adrenal gland?

Cortex: mesoderm
Medulla: neural crest cells
Which neural crest derivative forms the adrenal medulla?
Chromaffin cells of the adrenal medulla
What tumor arises from adrenal medulla chromaffin cells?
Pheochromocytoma
From which epithelium do gonads develop?
Coelomic epithelium (intermediate mesoderm)
What do Sertoli cells and Leydig cells arise from?

Sertoli cells: seminiferous tubule lining
Leydig cells: interstitial mesenchyme
What hormone secreted by Sertoli cells inhibits Müllerian duct development?
Müllerian inhibiting factor (MIF)
What hormone drives testicular differentiation from gonads embryologically?

Testes determining factor (TDF)
↳ Located on the Y Chromosome


🧠 Absence of TDF causes gonads → become ovaries.
Which structure guides descent of the ovaries? What ligaments does it eventually form?

Gubernaculum

↳  becomes the round ligament of the ovary and round ligament of the uterus
What is cryptorchidism and where does it take place?

Undescended testes
↳ commonly in iliac fossa or inguinal canal

 

~ Testes are in the illiac fossa at 8 months development
Why must cryptorchidism be corrected?

To prevent infertility and testicular cancer due to high intra-abdominal temperature



↳ via hyalinization and malignant tumors
What are the remnants of mesonephric duct tubules near the epididymis?
Efferent ductules and aberrant ductules (superior/inferior)
What male structures develop from the mesonephric (Wolffian) duct?
Epididymis, vas deferens, and ejaculatory duct
What structures develop from the paramesonephric (Müllerian) ducts?
Fallopian tubes, uterus, upper 1/3 of the vagina
How is the uterus formed embryologically?
By fusion of the lower paramesonephric ducts into the uterovaginal canal
What embryologic structure pulls the Müllerian ducts horizontally to form the fallopian tubes?
Developing myometrium contractions
What is the appendix of the testis? How is it embryologically formed?

A Müllerian duct remnant in males



~ Cystic formation
What is the appendix of the epididymis? How is it formed embryologically?

A mesonephric duct remnant above the epididymis



~ Cystic formation
What regulates ADH (vasopressin) synthesis and release?

Stimulated by increased Plasma osmolarity (hypertonic) and decreased Blood pressure or ↓ blood volume (low bp)

  
5/6 synthesized in Supraoptic nucleus
↳ 1/6 in Paraventricular nucleus


🧠 Remember, ADH = increased water retention (increase BP)
What does the primitive urogenital sinus divide into during development?
Vesicourethral canal and definitive urogenital sinus (DUGS)
What is exstrophy of the bladder (ectopia vesicae)?

Urinary bladder exposed due to failure of anterior abdominal wall closure. 



It is seen in conditions like Poland syndrome and prune belly syndrome
What embryologic structures give rise to the external female genitalia?

1) Genital tubercle → Clitoris
2) Genital folds → Labia minora
3) Genital swellings → Labia majora
What embryologic structures give rise to the external male genitalia?

1) Genital tubercle → Penis (glans and shaft)
2) Genital folds → Ventral shaft and penile urethra (merges with urethral plate)
3) Genital swellings → Scrotum
How does the penile urethra form in males?

The urethral plate elongates into the genital tubercle and canalizes, forming the urethral groove
↳ urethral folds fuse to enclose the penile urethra
What causes hypospadias and where is the urethral opening located?

Failure of urethral folds to fuse
↳  urethral opening is on the ventral (hypo-underside) surface of the penis
What causes epispadias and where is the urethral opening located? Embryologically?

Abnormal positioning of the genital tubercle
↳ urethral opening is on the dorsal (epi-upper) surface of the penis
What congenital syndrome is associated with epispadias and bladder exstrophy (ectopia vesicae)?
Prune belly syndrome (also associated with deficient abdominal muscles)
What is the glans penis and what covers it?

The glans is the distal expanded tip of the penis
↳ covered by the Prepuce (foreskin)
What is phimosis?
Inability to retract a tight foreskin over the glans penis
What is paraphimosis?

Retracted foreskin becomes trapped behind the glans, potentially constricting blood flow

 

~ Remember, dont confuse with phimosis
What causes male pseudohermaphroditism (46,XY with female external genitalia)?
Androgen insensitivity syndrome (defective testosterone receptors)
What causes female pseudohermaphroditism (46,XX with masculinized external genitalia)?
Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (excess androgen production)
What is true hermaphroditism?
The presence of both ovarian and testicular tissue (rare)
What are the remnants of the mesonephric tubules and ducts in females?

Epophoron (ductules, near ovary) and paraphoron (from mesonephric tubules)

Gartner’s duct (caudal mesonephric duct remnant).
What clinical relevance does Gartner’s duct have embryologically?
It may persist and form a Gartner's duct cyst in the lateral wall of the vagina
What artery passes over the ureter in females and what is the mnemonic?

Uterine artery passes over ureter

— “Water under the bridge.”
What are the terminal branches of the internal pudendal artery?

Deep and dorsal arteries of the penis/clitoris

 

Also inferior rectal
What is the function and composition of the superior and inferior hypogastric plexuses?

Superior hypogastric plexus: sympathetic (from lumbar splanchnic nerves) 
↳ located at aortic bifurcation

Inferior hypogastric plexus: mixed para and sympathetic
↳ located at lateral walls of true pelvis


Inferior hypogastric responsible for "Point and Shoot"
What type of autonomic innervation controls erection and ejaculation in the penis?

Erection = Parasympathetic (S2–S4)
Ejaculation = Somatic (pudendal nerve) + Sympathetic


🧠 Mnemonic: "Point (parasympathetic) and Shoot (sympathetic)"
What are the three zones of the adrenal cortex and their main hormone products?

Zona glomerulosa → aldosterone (mineralocorticoid)
Zona fasciculata → cortisol (glucocorticoid)
Zona reticularis → androgens (DHEA, androstenedione)



~ Remember, GFR = Glomerulosa, Fasciculata, Reticularis (inner)
G,F,R = Salt, Suger, Sex
What does the adrenal medulla secrete and what stimulates it?

It secretes epinephrine and norepinephrine (catecholamines)
↳ stimulated by sympathetic preganglionic neurons via acetylcholine
What is the rate-limiting step of steroid hormone synthesis in the adrenal cortex?

Conversion of cholesterol to pregnenolone
↳ by the enzyme desmolase (stimulated by ACTH)
What is the function of 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase in mineralocorticoid target tissues?

It converts cortisol to cortisone
↳ to prevent inappropriate activation of mineralocorticoid receptors by cortisol
What are the main actions of aldosterone in the kidney?

Increases Na⁺ reabsorption, K⁺ secretion
↳ and H⁺ secretion in the distal nephron

 

GFR - Salt, Sugar, Sex
What is the diurnal pattern of cortisol secretion?

Highest in early morning (~8 AM), lowest around midnight
↳ secreted in a pulsatile manner
Which enzyme activities require TPP (Thiamine B1) as a cofactor?

Pyruvate dehydrogenase (glycolysis → TCA)
α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase (TCA cycle)
Branched-chain α-ketoacid dehydrogenase (Maple syrup urine disease)
Transketolase (HMP shunt)
What are the classic deficiency syndromes of Thiamine (B1)?

1) Dry beriberi (peripheral neuropathy)
↳ Wet beriberi (cardiomyopathy/edema)
2) Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome (confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia, confabulation
What lab test helps diagnose thiamine deficiency?

Decreased erythrocyte transketolase activity
↳ HMP shunt (non-oxidative pathway)
What are the two coenzyme forms of Riboflavin (Vitamin B2)?
FMN (Flavin mononucleotide) and FAD (Flavin adenine dinucleotide)
What are the classic signs of Riboflavin (B2) deficiency?
Cheilosis, glossitis, corneal vascularization, seborrheic dermatitis
What are the coenzyme forms of Niacin (Vitamin B3)?
NAD⁺ and NADP
What is Pellagra and what are its "3 Ds"?

Pellagra is Niacin (B3) deficiency
↳ symptoms = Dermatitis, Diarrhea, Dementia
Which amino acid can be used to synthesize Niacin (B3), and what vitamin is required for this conversion?

Tryptophan
↳ requires Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine PLP)



~ Remember, Hartnup Disease
What is the pharmacologic use of Niacin (B3)?
Treats hyperlipidemia by lowering VLDL and raising HDL
What is the active form of Pantothenic acid (Vitamin B5), and what is its function?

Coenzyme A
↳ required for fatty acid metabolism and synthesis



Acetyl - "CoA" (Coenzyme A)
What are the active forms of Vitamin B6?
Pyridoxine, pyridoxal, pyridoxamine → converted to pyridoxal phosphate (PLP)
What metabolic reactions use Vitamin B6 (PLP) as a coenzyme?
Transamination, glycogenolysis, decarboxylation (e.g., GABA from glutamate)
Which drugs can cause Vitamin B6 deficiency?

Isoniazid (Tuberculosis treatment), oral contraceptives, penicillamine, chronic alcohol use



Symptoms: Cheilosis, glossitis, peripheral neuropathy, seborrheic dermatitis, Amemia
What is the role of Biotin (Vitamin B7)?

Cofactor for carboxylation reactions (e.g., pyruvate carboxylase, acetyl-CoA carboxylase)






Remember, FA synthesis
What can cause Biotin deficiency?

Raw egg white consumption (avidin binds biotin), long-term antibiotics



B7 for Beauty (alopecia, dermatitis, etc.)
What symptoms are associated with Biotin (B7) deficiency?

Dermatitis, alopecia, enteritis, ataxia, seizures


B7 = heaven = beauty = balding = alopecia
What differentiates the diagnosis of vitamin B6 deficiency from B2/B3 deficiency?

Anemia is a key feature of B6 (PLP) deficiency due to impaired heme synthesis (ALA synthase).


Also: Convulsions and irritability are more specific to B6.
B2 (riboflavin) → cheilosis, glossitis, corneal vascularization
B3 (niacin) → pellagra (dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia
What are key biochemical functions of Vitamin C (Ascorbic acid)?
Collagen synthesis (hydroxylation of Proline/Lysine), iron absorption, antioxidant, catecholamine synthesis
Why can’t humans synthesize Vitamin C?

Lack the enzyme L-gulonolactone oxidase



↳ Results in Scurvy (Bleeding gums, poor healing)
Which vitamin deficiency leads to seborrheic dermatitis and convulsions in infants on formula?

Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine)



~anemia in adults, convulsions in infants?
What is the active form of folic acid (vitamin B9)?

Tetrahydrofolate (THF)
 ↳  One-carbon transfers


Remember, Used in Pyrimadine synthesis (dUMP to dTMP)
What is folic acid (vitamin B9) made of structurally?

Pteridine ring + para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) 
 Pteroic acid → Conjugated to glutamate residues
What is the purpose of Folic Acid (B9) used in the body?

One-carbon transfers for reactions.
↳ Via Tetrahydrofolic acid (THF)

 

Purine synthesis → N¹⁰-formyl THF
TMP & Serine synthesis → N⁵,N¹⁰-methylene THF
Methionine synthesis (homocysteine) → N⁵-methyl THF
How does folate deficiency cause anemia?

Impaired DNA synthesis → defective cell division → large, immature erythroblasts
↳ Causes Megaloblastic anemia

 


Remember,
Folate (B9) → tetrahydrofolate (THF) → (Uracil nucleotide → Thymine nucleotide)
Which cells in a peripheral smear are diagnostic for folate deficiency?
Hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes), macro-ovalocytes
Why is it dangerous to treat a vitamin B12 deficiency with folate alone?
Folate can mask symptoms but worsen neurologic deficits.
What fetal abnormality is prevented by folic acid?

Neural tube defects (e.g., spina bifida)
↳ prevented with 0.4 mg/day supplementation during pregnancy




Folic acid = Vitamin B9 = Nine = Neurons
What are the two coenzyme forms of Vitamin B12?
Methylcobalamin and Deoxyadenosylcobalamin
What are the two major Vitamin B12-dependent reactions in humans?

1. Methylmalonyl-CoA → Succinyl-CoA (Odd-chain FA metabolism)
2. Homocysteine → Methionine (methionine metabolism)
What are the main metabolic effects of cortisol?

↑ gluconeogenesis, ↑ glycogen storage, ↑ lipolysis, ↑ insulin resistance, ↑ proteolysis

 

Also decreases inflammation (surpesses immune system)
~Basically lose muscle and lose fat and dont use glucose
What are cortisol’s anti-inflammatory effects?

- Inhibits phospholipase A2 (↓ prostoglandins)
- Suppresses cytokine production (e.g., IL-2)
- Reduces immune cell migration and activity
What are the immune effects of cortisol?

Decreased lymphocytes, eosinophils, basophils
Decreased antibody production

Increased neutrophils (due to demargination)
What are the effects of cortisol on connective tissue and bone?

↓ fibroblast activity = thin skin, poor wound healing

↓ bone formation, ↑ bone resorption = osteoporosis
What symptoms are seen in Addison’s disease (primary)?

Hypotension, hyperpigmentation, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, fatigue, weight loss, ↑ ACTH

 

Addisons = Adrenal gland = no aldosterone or cortisol (primary)
How does secondary adrenal insufficiency differ from primary?

No hyperpigmentation, no hyperkalemia (Aldosterone is normal) 



↳ Adrenal gland is fine, Pituitary is dysfunctional (due to ↓ ACTH)

Primary = adrenal problem
Secondary = pituitary problem
What are signs of Cushing’s syndrome (↑ cortisol)?
Central obesity, moon face, striae, hypertension, muscle wasting, osteoporosis, glucose intolerance
What is the cause and classic triad of pheochromocytoma?

Catecholamine-secreting tumor (usually adrenal medulla)
↳ triad: episodic headache, sweating, tachycardia/HTN


 


Remember, VMA diagnostic test for diagnosing pheochromocytoma (RNS module)
What lab markers are used to measure cortisol and androgen secretion?

17-OH steroids (cortisol)
17-ketosteroids (androgens)
What is Conn’s syndrome and what are its features?

Primary hyperaldosteronism (adenoma)
↳ hypertension, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis and suppressed renin

 

Secondary = ↑ Aldosterone AND ↑ Renin
What is the defect in 21β-hydroxylase deficiency and its clinical effect?

↓ cortisol and aldosterone, ↑ androgens 
↳ virilization (females), salt-wasting, hypotension.



21β = ↑ androgens
What is the functional unit of the thyroid gland?

The thyroid follicle 
↳ consists of colloid surrounded by follicular epithelial cells
Which cells produce thyroid hormones?
Thyroid follicular cells (simple cuboidal epithelium in active state)
What do parafollicular (C) cells (thyroid) secrete?

Calcitonin
↳ which lowers blood calcium (inhibiting osteoclast activity)


 

Increases phosphate and Ca2+ excretion

"Calcitonin" = calcium Not-IN
What changes are seen in follicular cells during high thyroid activity?
Epithelium becomes columnar, colloid becomes scanty
What stimulates the Na⁺/I⁻ symporter in thyroid cells?

TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone) from the anterior pituitary



~ TRH promotes TSH from pituitary
What enzyme is responsible for oxidation and organification of iodide in the thyroid?
Thyroid peroxidase (TPO)
What is formed when monoiodotyrosine (MIT) and (diiodotyrosine) DIT are coupled?

MIT + DIT = T3

DIT + DIT = T4
What is the most biologically active thyroid hormone?
T3 (Triiodothyronine), binds nuclear receptors
What are the lab serum findings in primary hypothyroidism?

↑TSH, ↓ Free T4

Thyroid gland defective, doesnt produce enough T3/T4

 

↑TSH from pituitary
↓ from thyroid (disfunctional)
What autoimmune condition is associated with hypothyroidism and anti-TPO antibodies?

Hashimoto’s thyroiditis 



Increased serum DIT and MIT
What condition presents with exophthalmos and is caused by TSI antibodies?

Graves’ disease (hyperthyroidism)


Low TRH, Low TSH,  High T3/T4
TSI antibodies = TSH receptor antibodies

Exophthalmos = Bulging eyes
What are the histologic changes seen in Graves’ disease?

Papillary infoldings, scalloped colloid, hyperplastic follicular cells

 


~Epithelium becomes super columnar
What is cretinism? What hormone deficiency causes it?

Congenital hypothyroidism causing irreversible CNS damage and growth delay



Causes mental Retardation
What cells are responsible for secreting PTH?
Chief cells of the parathyroid gland
What is the function of PTH on the kidney?

Increases serum calcium by stimulating bone resorption, renal Ca²⁺ reabsorption, and vitamin D activation (1α-hydroxylase)

 

Ca2+ reabsorption 
Pi excretion
What are oxyphil cells and where/why do they appear?

Acidophilic, larger cells of unknown function (parathyroid gland)
↳ appear after puberty
What histologic feature indicates a hypoactive thyroid follicle?
Lining epithelium is flattened (squamous), colloid is abundant
What are the boundaries of the Perineum?

Anterior: pubic symphysis
Anterolateral: ischiopubic rami
Lateral: ischial tuberosities
Posterolateral: sacrotuberous ligaments
Posterior: coccyx



"Perineos" meaning: "region between the anus and the genitals"
What triangles subdivide the Perineum?
Urogenital triangle (anterior) and Anal triangle (posterior)
What are the contents of the superficial perineal pouch in females?

Bulbospongiosus, ischiocavernosus, superficial transverse perineal muscles


Bulbs of vestibule, greater/lesser vestibular glands
What structure separates the superficial and deep perineal pouches?
Perineal membrane
What are the erectile tissues of the female perineum?
Clitoris (corpora cavernosa) and bulbs of vestibule (corpus spongiosum homologs)
What gland in females is homologous to the bulbourethral gland in males?
Bartholin glands (greater vestibular glands)
What gland in females is homologous to the prostate gland?
Skene glands (lesser vestibular glands)
What innervates the external anal sphincter?
Inferior rectal nerve (branch of pudendal nerve, somatic S2–S4)
What innervates the internal anal sphincter?
Autonomic (sympathetic via inferior hypogastric plexus)
What muscles compress erectile tissues during ejaculation?

What drug is a recombinant PTH analog that builds bone when used intermittently?

Teriparatide
↳ Continuous PTH → Catabolic 
↳ Intermittent PTH → Anabolic 

~ used in small doses treatment of osteoporosis

"teri-" → drug name (manufacturer)
"para-" → from parathyroid
"-tide" → suffix for peptides 
Teriparatide = synthetic peptide analog of PTH
What is secreted by PP cells of the pancreas?
Pancreatic polypeptide (inhibits exocrine pancreas, slows GI absorption)
What transporter allows glucose to enter β-cells (pancreas)?

GLUT2 (insulin-independent).

 


~Has very high Km (15-20 mM), meaning it only uptakes glucose when blood glucose levels are high

(Remember GLUT5 is fructose in intestines)
Where is the urinary bladder located in adults vs. children?

In adults, it’s in the lesser pelvis 
↳ in children, it’s mostly abdominal
What anchors the neck of the bladder in males?

Puboprostatic ligaments (connect bladder neck to pubic bones)
↳ Pubovesicle ligaments for women
What is the trigone of the bladder and why is it clinically important?

Smooth mucosal area between ureteric orifices & internal urethral orifice
↳ Embryologically from mesonephric ducts
 ↳ important site for infections
Which nerve fibers control the detrusor muscle and internal urethral sphincter?

Parasympathetic (Pelvic nerve, S2–S4): Contracts detrusor & relaxes internal sphincter for micturition.
Sympathetic (Hypogastric nerve, T11–L2): Contracts internal sphincter to prevent semen reflux during ejaculation.
What crosses over the ureter in males?

The vas deferens 
↳ remembered as “Water under the bridge
What is the main secretion of the seminal vesicles?
Alkaline fluid with fructose (energy for sperm) + coagulating agents
What is the main secretion of the prostate gland?
Thin, milky fluid that activates and protects sperm.
Which prostate zone is the most common site of cancer?
Peripheral zone (posterolateral – palpable on DRE)
Which prostate zone is the most common site of BPH (benign prostatic hyperplasia)?
Transitional zone (causes urethral compression)
What venous plexus connects the prostate to the spine, allowing cancer to metastasize?

Prostatic venous plexus 
↳ communicates with Batson Vertebral venous plexus
What type of tumor is the most common solid cancer in young men (15–35 y/o)?
Testicular cancer (often seminoma)
Which nerve is damaged in loss of erection after prostate surgery?

Pelvic splanchnic nerves (S2–S4) 
↳ parasympathetic fibers responsible for erection
↳ (prostatic plexus if given the answer)


Remember, Point and Shoot
What does the prostate secrete and why is it important?

Milky alkaline secretion (30% semen volume) 

↳ containing citric acid, fibrinolysin (liquefies semen), zinc, and acid phosphatase
What are corpora amylacea in the prostate?
Calcified secretions that increase with age
Which prostate zone is associated with Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH) and which with cancer?

Transitional zone (middle lobe) → BPH (no tenderness)

Peripheral zone (posterior lobe) → adenocarcinoma (posterior/palpable)
What do bulbourethral (Cowper’s) glands produce?

Pre-ejaculate 
↳ mucus, galactose, sialic acid.
Describe the epithelial transition in the male urethra.

Transitional epithelium (proximal)
→ pseudostratified/stratified columnar 
   → stratified squamous Non-keratinized (distal)


Cuboidal to columnar to stratified squamous
What tissue surrounds the corpora cavernosa and corpus spongiosum?
Tunica albuginea (dense fibrous sheath)
What are the four layers of the ovary?
Germinal epithelium, tunica albuginea, cortex (with follicles), medulla (vascular loose CT)
What hormone prevents follicular atresia?

Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH).


Follicular atresia = degeneration
What triggers ovulation on day 14?
The LH surge
Which ovarian follicle is present before puberty?
Primordial follicle (primary oocyte + flat follicular cells)
What are the functions of granulosa lutein vs. theca lutein cells?

Granulosa lutein → progesterone
Theca lutein → estrogen

 

~ Granulosa and theca interna becomes granulosa lutein and theca lutein
What is the function of progesterone and estrogen post ovulation?

Progesterone from granulosa lutein cells stabilizes the endometrium for implantation

Estrogen from theca lutein helps build the uterine lining


Post ovulation, granulosa produces progesterone and theca producers estrogen
What are the key steps in insulin secretion after glucose enters β-cells?

1) Glycolysis makes ATP
2) ATP Closes K⁺ channels 
3) Depolarization → opens Ca²⁺ channels
4)  insulin exocytosis
How do sulfonylureas (e.g., glyburide) stimulate insulin secretion?

Increase insulin release (agonist)
↳ Block ATP-sensitive K⁺ channels on β-cells



Sulfonylureas mimics increased ATP levels
 ↳ e.g. glyburide, glipizide, glimepiride
What type of receptor is the insulin receptor?

Tyrosine kinase receptor 
↳ α-subunits bind insulin
↳ β-subunits have TK activity
Which glucose transporters are insulin-dependent vs insulin-independent?

Insulin-dependent: 
↳ GLUT4 (muscle, fat)

Insulin-independent: 
↳ GLUT1 (RBCs, brain)
↳ GLUT2 (liver, β-cells)
↳ GLUT3 (neurons)
What are the carbohydrate, protein, and lipid effects of insulin?

Carbohydrates: ↑ glycogenesis and ↓ glycogenolysis

Protein: ↑ protein synthesis and ↓ proteolysis

Lipids: ↑ lipogenesis
Which tissues uptake glucose without insulin? (Board favorite)
Brain, RBCs, liver, kidney, intestine, β-cells
What is the effect of insulin on potassium?

Drives K⁺ into cells
↳ can cause hypokalemia 



(insulin + glucose to treat hyperkalemia)
What stimulates glucagon secretion?

Hypoglycemia, amino acids, catecholamines



(And starving lol)
What second messenger does glucagon use?
cAMP (Gs protein-coupled receptor)
What hormone inhibits both insulin and glucagon?
Somatostatin
What are the 2 main types of diabetes mellitus?

Type 1 (autoimmune β-cell destruction, absolute insulin deficiency) 
↳ People born with it

Type 2 (insulin resistance, relative deficiency)
↳ maturity onset
Which type of diabetes is associated with ketoacidosis?

Type 1 
↳ DKA (Metabolic Acidosis, high ketones, high glucose)


Buzzwords: fruity acetone breath, Kussmaul respirations

Remember, type 2 rarely causes ketoacidosis
Which HLA types are linked to Type 1 diabetes?

HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR4 



are human leukocyte antigen (HLA) genes that are associated with increased risk of type 1 diabetes.
What is hyperinsulinism and what can it cause?

Excess insulin (e.g., insulinoma, overdose)

↳ Can cause hypoglycemia, seizures, coma
What are the 4 main counter-regulatory hormones that raise blood glucose?

Glucagon, Cortisol, GH, Epinephrine


(opposite of insulin)
What are the hallmark long-term complications of diabetes mellitus?
Microvascular (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) & macrovascular (atherosclerosis).
What is the typical age of puberty onset in girls vs boys, and how long does it last?

Girls: 10–13 yrs
Boys: 13–15 yrs

↳ duration 2–4.5 yrs.
What neuroendocrine event triggers puberty?
Gradual reactivation of pulsatile GnRH secretion from the hypothalamus
What hormone from adipose tissue signals adequate energy stores for puberty?

Leptin (permits pulsatile GnRH secretion)
 
  

~ reflects the amount of body fat stored.
What adrenal event precedes puberty and contributes to pubic/axillary hair?

Adrenarche 
↳ maturation of adrenal zona reticularis (androgens) 

 

Causes Increase in DHEA/DHEAS (androgens)
How does melatonin influence puberty?

High melatonin inhibits GnRH
↳ decline at puberty removes inhibition (pinealectomy → precocious puberty)



Remember, high melatonin in childhood inhibits GnRH release
What is aromatase, what reaction does it catalyze?
Conversion of androgens (e.g., testosterone) into estrogens (e.g., estradiol, estrone)
What is the order of events in female puberty?

Thelarche → Pubarche → Menarche



Note, FSH and LH peak in fetus stage because all production of eggs during gestation.

Thel- = “breast” 
-arche = begin

Pub- = pubic hair
-arche = begin

men = month
-arche = begin
Are initial menstrual cycles ovulatory?

No 
↳ they are anovulatory



 Regular ovulation occurs ~1 year later.
What is the first sign of puberty in boys?
Testicular enlargement (↑ Leydig testosterone)
Which hormone predominates over FSH in late puberty and adulthood?
LH > FSH (FSH suppressed by inhibin)
What hormone predominates in childhood & menopause?

Childhood: FSH > LH
Menopause: FSH > LH
Why are men typically taller than women?

Pubertal growth spurt starts later in boys + androgens close epiphyses slower than estrogens 

↳  ~10 cm taller on average
What are Tanner stages used for?
To assess sexual maturity based on breast/genital development and pubic hair
Define True (central) vs Pseudo precocious puberty.

True: Early but normal GnRH activation.
Pseudo: Sex steroid ↑ without GnRH (e.g., CAH, tumors).



If LH/FSH are low and sex steroids are high → pseudo
When is puberty considered delayed?

Girls: No menarche by 17
Boys: No testicular enlargement by 20
What syndrome causes delayed puberty with anosmia?
Kallmann syndrome (GnRH neuron migration defect)
What is the hormonal profile in Kallmann syndrome?
↓ GnRH, LH, FSH, Testosterone (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism).
What is female pseudohermaphroditism and its most common cause?

46,XX with ovaries + virilized genitalia

↳ most commonly due to CAH (21β-hydroxylase deficiency)
↳ Congenital adrenal hyperplasia
What is the fate of the corpus luteum if no fertilization occurs?
Degenerates into corpus albicans
What is the site of fertilization?
Ampulla of the fallopian tube
What are the two main cell types in the fallopian tube epithelium?

Ciliated cells (move ovum) 
Peg (non-ciliated) cells (secretory, nourish gametes).

 

simple columnar epithelium
What are the three layers of the uterus?
Perimetrium, myometrium, endometrium
Which layer of the endometrium is shed during menstruation?

The functional layer (stratum functionalis).
↳ endometrium
Which arteries supply the functional vs. basal layer of the endometrium?

Spiral arteries → functional layer
Straight arteries → basal layer
What causes myometrial hypertrophy and gap junction formation during pregnancy?
Estrogen and progesterone
What epithelium lines the endocervix?
Simple columnar mucus-secreting epithelium
What epithelium lines the ectocervix?
Stratified squamous non-keratinized epithelium
Where does cervical cancer usually arise?

The transformation zone (squamocolumnar junction).
↳ External os

 

Linked to HPV types 16 and 18.
What epithelium lines the vagina?
Stratified squamous non-keratinized epithelium
What CNS defect commonly causes true precocious puberty?

Hypothalamic hamartoma
⤷ GnRH Thalamus loss
 

(non-cancerous tumor)
What hormone causes regression of Müllerian ducts in the male fetus?

Müllerian Inhibitory Factor (MIF/AMH) 
↳ secreted by fetal Sertoli cells
Which hormone is required for virilization of external genitalia?

Dihydrotestosterone (DHT) 
↳ converted from testosterone by 5α-reductase

 

~ and prostate gland
What hormone is responsible for development of the Wolffian ducts (internal male genitalia)?

Testosterone (not DHT)



~ not including Prostate gland

Testosterone = internal development (& penis enlargement)
DHT = external development
Which cells form the blood-testis barrier and produce inhibin?
Sertoli cells
What is the function of inhibin?

Selectively inhibits FSH secretion from the anterior pituitary.

 

Inhibin B (from Sertoli cells in males & granulosa cells / corpus luteum in females) inhibits FSH secretion from anterior pituitary (negative feedback).
What temperature condition is essential for normal spermatogenesis?
Testes must be 1–2°C below core body temperature (scrotal position)
Which androgen is 30–50× more potent than testosterone?
Dihydrotestosterone (DHT).
What feedback effects do testosterone and estrogens have on the HPT axis?
Negative feedback on GnRH, LH, and FSH secretion.
What medication inhibits 5α-reductase and what is it used for?

Finasteride 
↳ treats BPH & male-pattern baldness
What results from dysfunction of the tight junctions (blood-testis barrier) in the testis?

Anti-sperm antibodies infiltrate seminiferous tubules

↳ Causes autoimmune orchitis (immune attack on sperm)
What happens to spermatogenesis if testicular temperature increases?

It is inhibited.

↳ Cryptorchidism leads to infertility due to tubular degeneration.
How is testosterone transported in the blood?

60% bound to Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin (SHBG)
38% to albumin
2% free (active form).
What are signs of primary hypogonadism?
Low sperm count (<40 million/mL),  ↑ LH/FSH, testicular dysfunction
What distinguishes secondary hypogonadism?
↓ GnRH/LH/FSH, ↓ sperm count, may be due to pituitary or hypothalamic issue
What happens in male hypogonadism before puberty?
Eunuchism: delayed puberty, tall stature, infertility, no secondary sex traits
What happens in male hypogonadism after puberty?
↓ libido, infertility, regression of secondary sex traits, hot flashes
What are the phases of male sexual response?
Erection (PNS), Emission (SNS), Ejaculation (somatic), Resolution
When and where does oogenesis arrest during fetal development?
Oocytes arrest in prophase I of meiosis during fetal development and remain as primary oocytes until puberty
What triggers the completion of Meiosis I in the oocyte?

LH surge just before ovulation
 


Remember, arrested in meiosis I before puberty
Which cells in the ovary have LH receptors and synthesize androgens?

Theca cells

 

Cholesterol → Androgen (testosterone)
↳ no aromatase
Which cells in the ovary have FSH receptors and convert androgens to estrogen?

Granulosa cells (via aromatase)

 

Androgens (testosterone) → Estrogen
What is Mittelschmerz?

Mid-cycle pain due to follicular rupture during ovulation



German:
Mittel = “middle”
Schmerz = “pain"
What hormones are secreted by the corpus luteum?
Progesterone, estrogen, and inhibin
What rescues the corpus luteum during early pregnancy?
hCG (from syncytiotrophoblast)
When is there positive feedback of estrogen on LH and FSH?
Late follicular phase (just before ovulation)
What causes menstruation if fertilization does not occur?
Progesterone withdrawal due to corpus luteum degeneration
What enzyme is released by theca cells to help LH surge follicular rupture?
Collagenase
What phase of the ovarian cycle coincides with menstruation?
Early follicular phase
What drug induces ovulation by blocking estrogen feedback at the hypothalamus?
Clomiphene (SERM)
What hormone causes thick, sticky cervical mucus post-ovulation?

Progesterone


Thin, clear mucus = estrogen effect (fertile)
Thick, sticky mucus = progesterone effect (post-ovulation, non-fertile)
What is the best lab marker for menopause?

↑↑ FSH (serum)



Premenopause is detected via increased serum FSH levels
What is the source of estrogen in postmenopausal women?

Peripheral conversion of androgens to estrone



~ In adipose tissue (peripheral tissues)
What are the effects of progesterone on the respiratory system?

Respiratory stimulant, increasing the sensitivity of CO₂ and promoting hyperventilation
↳  Deacreased PaCO₂ (especially in pregnancy)
What does “Spinnbarkeit” refer to?
Stretchy, stringy cervical mucus due to estrogen at ovulation
What causes heavy menstrual bleeding (>80 mL or >7 days)?

Menorrhagia 
↳ hormone imbalance, fibroids, IUDs
What is premature ovarian failure?

Menopause-like symptoms < age 40 with ↓ estrogen, ↑ FSH/LH



i.e. early menopause
What is the role of the zona reaction during fertilization?

Prevents polyspermy 
↳ modifying ZP3 glycoproteins after the first sperm enters
Which hormone is the basis for pregnancy tests and when does it appear?

hCG
↳ detectable in blood by day 8–9 post-ovulation (fertilization).
What are the functions of hCG in early pregnancy?
Maintains corpus luteum, stimulates fetal testes to produce testosterone
Which placental hormone mimics growth hormone and has anti-insulin effects?
hPL (human placental lactogen) or hCS (human chorionic somatomammotropin)
What maternal metabolic changes are caused by hPL?

↑ Lipolysis, ↑ ketogenesis, ↓ glucose utilization 
↳  can lead to gestational diabetes

 

Mimics Growth Hormone effects
↳ Maternal hyperglycemia
How does vitamin K help in coagulation?

Changes in amino acid of coagulation proteins (enable calcium binding)
↳  γ-Glutamyl carboxylase


 

(an example of post-translational modification)
What clotting factors are Vitamin K involved in?

Factors II, VII, IX, X

 

Inhibited by warfarin

B12 = Blood tests reveal propionic acid, Methylmalonic acid and lactic acid
What Water soluble Vitamin deficiencies can cause homocystinuria? (methionine metabolism)
Vitamins B6 (PLP) , B9 and B12
What water soluble Vitamin is involved in increased protein intake, glycogenolysis, and loss of memory?
Vitamin B6 (PLP)

Fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL

Stage IA means confined to the cervix  (depth < 3 mm)

 

I = In cervix
II = Into vagina (upper part) or parametrium
III = To pelvic wall or lower vagina
IV = Invades bladder/rectum or distant spread
Anterior vaginal wall collapse, urinary incontinence with positive Q-tip test (>30°) — most likely diagnosis?

Urethrocele (urethral prolapse)
↳ Due to weakened pelvic floor muscles after birth



Positive Q-tip test (greater than 30) confirms urethra displacement

Key symptom: Stress urinary incontinence (leakage with cough, sneeze, exertion)
What is a common cause of perineal pain in athletes (e.g., cyclists) that is worsened by sitting and relieved by standing?

Pudendal Neuralgia (S2–S4)
↳ Compression of the pudendal nerve in Alcock’s canal
A deep laceration near the inguinal canal would damage which structure in females? In males?

Round ligament of the uterus (Female)
Spermatic Cord (Male)

 

~ Also ilioinguinal nerve
What is a Culdocentesis and what is it used for?

Procedure to access the rectouterine pouch
↳ Suspected ruptured ectopic pregnancy



Posterior fornix is thinnest part of vaginal wall and closely related to peritoneum
What is the normal Uterus anatomical Position? What are its angles?

Anteverted (90°–100° tilted forward)
&
Anteflexed (160°–170° bent forward)
What is the heirarchy for uterus support structures in the body (first, second, third)

1. Pelvic floor muscles (levator ani muscle group) – primary support
2. Uterosacral & cardinal ligaments – secondary support
3. Other minor ligaments (e.g., round ligament, broad ligament) - tertiary support
Which scrotal layer is derived from the external oblique aponeurosis?
External Spermatic Fascia
What is the nerve pathway for erection in males?

Pelvic Splanchnic Nerves (S2–S4) - parasympathetic 
→ Inferior Hypogastric Plexus
→ Prostatic Plexus
→ Cavernous Nerves
→ Corpus Cavernosum Tissue (vasodilation → erection)
Where does the Testicular artery directly branch off from?

Abdominal aorta (at L2)
↳ just below the renal arteries
How is fecal continence maintained?

Decreasing anorectal angle (pulls rectum forward)
↳ Puborectalis muscle contracts
What can cause severe headache after a lumbar puncture?
CSF leakage causing hypotension in the cranium
Which ovarian cells form the follicle? Which are connective tissue?

Granulosa cells – Follicle lining cells (support oocyte)

Theca cells = Connective tissue cells producing androgens
What are the histological stages of follicle development?

Primordial follicle → Single squamous follicular cells around primary oocyte
Primary follicle → Cuboidal granulosa cells
Secondary (antral) follicle → Multiple granulosa layers, Antrum (space) forms, theca interna/externa form
Mature (Graafian) follicle → Large antrum, oocyte on cumulus oophorus, surrounded by corona radiatals
What is the final stage of follicle development
Graffian (mature) follicle
What are the three layers sperm must cross to fertilize an oocyte?

Corona radiata – outermost layer.
Zona pellucida – thick glycoprotein shell (sperm binding acrosome reaction).
Oocyte plasma membrane (oolemma) - fusion of sperm and egg 




Mnemonic: Cute Zebras Ovulate
(Corona radiata → Zona pellucida → Oolemma)
How does the secretory phase of the uterine cycle appear histologically, and what causes it?

Appearance: Saw-toothed (serrated) glands with secretions in the lumen
Hormone: Progesterone from the corpus luteum


 

Day 15 to day 25

What hormones are essential for parturition (birth)?

Estrogen → increases myometrial oxytocin receptor expression and gap junctions.
Oxytocin → causes uterine contractions (positive feedback loop).
Prostaglandins → soften and dilate the cervix, enhance
What Compound levels are found in higher levels in Hashimoto’s thyroiditis?

Increased Serum DIT levels
↳ Thyroid follicle rupture

 

Anti-thyroglobulin & anti-TPO antibodies → follicule destruction
→ release of stored iodinated compounds like DIT and MIT.

Remember,
Graves’ disease has increased in anti-TSH receptor antibodies and t3/t4
What are the systemic BP changes seen in thyrotoxicosis (hyperthyroidism)?

↑ Systolic BP, ↓ Diastolic BP, ↑ Pulse pressure



Increases cardiac contractility and heart rate (↑ systolic BP) while vasodilation lowers diastolic BP, widening the pulse pressure
What is Lactational amenorrhea, and what impact does it have on hormone levels?

Temporary absence of menstruation (amenorrhea) that occurs post-birth during breastfeeding

 

Suckling stimulates prolactin, which inhibits GnRH
↳ This inhibits FSH and LH (prevents ovulation)
What are the serum Calcium, PTH, and Phosphate levels in Pseudohypoparathyroidism?

Low Ca2+
High Pi 
High PTH (Organ resistance)
How Does Sildenafil citrate enhance erections in males?

Inhibits phosphodiestrase-5 
↳ enhancing smooth muscle relaxation (vasodilation)
What part of the male sexual response is most impacted by Beta-Blockers?
Emission (Sympathetic NS)
What is the main physical difference between the female and male pelvis?
Female pelvises subpubic angle is 90° or greater
What are the dual innervations of the scrotum?

Genitofemoral nerve & posterior femoral cutaneous nerve



Anterior scrotum: genitofemoral nerve and illioinguinal nerve (L1)
Posterior scrotum: pudendal nerve and posterior femoral cutaneous nerve (S3)
Which maternal or fetal hormone precursor is a precurser for estradiol?
DHEA-S from fetal adrenal glands
What would administering insulin via IV do to Growth Hormone, Glucagon, and Epinephrine levels?

↑ GH, ↑ glucagon, ↑ epinephrine



Hypoglycemia stimulates: GH, glucagon, and epinephrine (stress response)
Whats the difference between cortical reaction and zona reaction?

Cortical reaction is the release of ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺ in the oocyte.

This feeds to the Zona reaction which Proteolysis and chemical modification of ZP3 glycoprotein to prevent sperm binding
What inhibits milk production during pregnancy?

High levels of progesterone (From the placenta)
↳ inhibit prolactin’s action on alveolar cells (Breast)



~Dont confuse with dopamine
What are abortion pills mechanism of action? How does it compare to OCPs?

Abortion pills contain Progesterone antagonists that lead to the decline of the endometrium lining.
↳ e.g. RU486 (mifepristone)



OCPs apply Negative feedback on hypothalamus/pituitary to prevent FSH & LH surge (no ovulation)
Whats the difference between Transient and Permanent Central Diabetes Insipidus?

Transient = temporary, damage to the posterior pituitary (can regenerate)

Permanent = damage to the hypothalamic nuclei (supraoptic)
What condition presents fully present female secondary sexual characteristics but amenorrhea and rudimentary uterus?

Müllerian agenesis syndrome (MRKH-syndrome)

↳ Müllerian (paramesonephric) ducts fail to fuse or develop completely
What artery/ligament provides blood supply to the female ovary?

Ovarian artery travels within the infundibulopelvic ligament (suspensory ligament of ovary)



~ Can twist and cause ovarian torsion
Why is proteinuria normal in pregnancy?

Increased blood & cardiac output from child causes increased GFR
↳ Leads to trace proteinuria (<300 mg/24hr)
What is adenomyosis?

Glands and stroma growth in the myometrium instead of endometrium



Endometriosis = ectopic endometrial tissue outside uterus.
Adenomyosis = ectopic endometrial tissue within myometrium

Which scrotal layer does hydrocele accumulate fluid in?

Tunica Vaginalis

 


A hydrocele is a collection of serous fluid between the parietal & visceral layers of the tunica vaginalis
Which tissue can only use glucose as an energy source?

RBCs (erythrocytes)
↳ lack mitochondria


Remember, Brain can use ketones for 2/3 of activities (during starving state)
What would exogenous testosterone do to LH, estrogen, and sperm content?

Serum LH: ↓ (negative feedback)
Serum estrogen: ↑ (aromatization of excess testosterone)
Spermatogenesis: ↓ (suppressed FSH from LH)
Whats the embryological difference in formation between epispadias and hypospadias in males?

Hypospadias = Urethral fold failure (ventral urethra)

Epispadias = Genitle tubercle failure (dorsal urethra)
Why can women still get pregnant immediately after a male has a vasectomy?
Stored sperm beyond the vasectomy site remain viable for months
What the difference between Pulsatile GnRH treatment vs Continuous GnRH treatment?

Pulsatile GnRH: stimulates anterior pituitary to release LH & FSH 
↳promotes ovulation in women and spermatogenesis in men

Continuous GnRH: suppresses LH & FSH (due to receptor downregulation)
↳ used for things like prostate cancer, endometriosis, and precocious puberty



~ pulsatile for fertility, continuous for suppression
What hormone levels would indicate sheehan syndrome?
Prolactin ↓ FSH ↓ TSH ↓ (pituitary necrosis)
What age of pregnancy does the placenta take over for hCG, progesterone, and estriol production?

8-10 weeks

↳ oophorectomy (ovary removal) doesnt impact hormone levels at this stage
